coredata.dc:title: Zero-dimensional N-doped carbon quantum dots and palladium co-modified titanium electrodes enhance the electrocatalytic degradation of oxytetracycline hydrochloride
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jian
Ren, Junlian
Wang, Fanjin
Zhang, Lanhe
Wang, Bing
He, Weihua
coredata.dc:description: Reducing the amount of precious metals and improving their catalytic activities are difficult problems in the field of electrocatalysis. In this study, Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes were prepared by introducing the N element into zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots and then compounded with Pd metal, and the electron transfer mechanism was explored. The results showed that the composite electrode degraded OTC up to 90.41% (60 min) at natural pH with an energy consumption of only 0.288 kWh/m3. The Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode formed a Pd-N-CQDs interface due to the electronic metal-carbon interaction (EMCI). In this, electrons are transferred from Pd to N, and the Pd-N interface plays a major role in hydrogen precipitation and the generation of atomic hydrogen. The generated H* ,·OH, and HClO all contribute to the degradation of OTC. According to the cycling experiments, the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode showed good stability. When applied to OTC degradation in real water bodies, good reactivity was still maintained. Toxicity assessment of the degradation intermediates by ECOSAR and TEST showed that the electrode could effectively reduce the biotoxicity of OTC. In this study, the interface of the catalytic electrode was tuned to enhance its intrinsic activity, which provides an efficient strategy for electrocatalytic remediation of antibiotic contamination.

(a) XRD patterns of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode, (b) XPS measured spectra of the composite electrode (inset shows the structure of N-CQDs), (c) C 1 s, (d) O 1 s, (e) N 1 s and (f) Pd 3d XPS narrow scan patterns.

(a) Impedance diagram of Ti substrate, N-CQDs/Ti support electrode, and Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode; inset shows the equivalent circuit diagram (0.5 mol/L Na2SO4, Frequency range = 105∼10−2), (b) polarization curve diagram (0.5 mol/L Na2SO4, Scan rate = 0.167 mV/s), (c) LSV curve (0.5 mol/L Na2SO4, Scan rate = 50 mV/s, Scan area = −1.5∼−0.3 V), (d) cyclic voltammogram (0.5 mol/L Na2SO4, Scan rate = 50 mV/s, Scan area = −0.6 to 1.0 V).

Effect of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode preparation conditions on OTC degradation: (a) N-CQDs concentration, (b) reaction kinetics of OTC degradation at electrodes prepared with different N-CQDs concentrations, (c) PdCl2 concentration, (d) reaction kinetics of OTC degradation at electrodes prepared with different PdCl2 concentrations, (e) comparison of degradation effect at different electrodes, (f) reaction kinetics of OTC degradation at different electrodes.

Factors affecting OTC degradation at Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode: (a) electrolyte NaCl concentration (I=5 mA, [OTC]=20 mg/L, pH=4.5, 35 °C), (b) degradation kinetics at different NaCl concentrations, (c) degradation current ([NaCl]=0.40 mol/L, [OTC]=20 mg/L, pH=4.5, 35 °C), (d) reaction kinetics at different degradation currents, (e) initial OTC concentration; (I=9 mA, [NaCl]=0.40 mol/L, pH=4.5, 35 °C) insets show k values at different initial OTC concentrations, (f) zero charge point diagram, (g) initial pH of the solution and k values at different pH, (I=9 mA, [NaCl]=0.40 mol/L, [OTC]=20 mg/L, 35 °C), (h) reaction temperature (I=9 mA, [NaCl]=0.40 mol/L, [OTC]=20 mg/L, pH=4.5), (i) the comparison plot of degradation time, degradation efficiency, and Pd concentration for different electrodes in the literature.

(a) Ten consecutive degradation experiments, (I=9 mA, [NaCl]=0.40 mol/L, [OTC]=20 mg/L, pH=4.5, 25 °C), (b) comparison of XRD spectra of the electrode before and after degradation, (c) effect of different antibiotic degradation ([TC]=[TYL]=[DOX]=[NOR]=[SMX]=20 mg/L, I=9 mA, [NaCl]=0.40 mol/L, pH=4.5, 25 °C), and (d) effect of OTC degradation in real water.

(a) Coumarin fluorescence spectrum of·OH, (b) quenching experiment, (c) LSV curve(0.5 mol/L Na2SO4, Scan rate = 50 mV/s, Scan area = 0–2 V), (d) concentration of hydrogen peroxide, (e) reaction mechanism diagram of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode, (f) possible degradation pathway of OTC.
(a) Acute toxicity of OTC and its degradation products to fish, daphnia, and green algae, (b) chronic toxicity, (c) bioconcentration factor, (d) developmental toxicity, and (e) mutagenicity.
Comparison of the degradation efficiency of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode with other catalysts for OTC.
Comparison of energy consumption of different electrodes for pollutant degradation.

head.ce:title: Zero-dimensional N-doped carbon quantum dots and palladium co-modified titanium electrodes enhance the electrocatalytic degradation of oxytetracycline hydrochloride

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reducing the amount of precious metals and improving their catalytic activities are difficult problems in the field of electrocatalysis. In this study, Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes were prepared by introducing the N element into zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots and then compounded with Pd metal, and the electron transfer mechanism was explored. The results showed that the composite electrode degraded OTC up to 90.41% (60 min) at natural pH with an energy consumption of only 0.288 kWh/m3. The Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode formed a Pd-N-CQDs interface due to the electronic metal-carbon interaction (EMCI). In this, electrons are transferred from Pd to N, and the Pd-N interface plays a major role in hydrogen precipitation and the generation of atomic hydrogen. The generated H* ,·OH, and HClO all contribute to the degradation of OTC. According to the cycling experiments, the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode showed good stability. When applied to OTC degradation in real water bodies, good reactivity was still maintained. Toxicity assessment of the degradation intermediates by ECOSAR and TEST showed that the electrode could effectively reduce the biotoxicity of OTC. In this study, the interface of the catalytic electrode was tuned to enhance its intrinsic activity, which provides an efficient strategy for electrocatalytic remediation of antibiotic contamination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTC) is a broad-spectrum tetracycline antibiotic that is commonly used in aquaculture and animal husbandry[1,2]. However, OTC cannot be completely absorbed by animals, and about 90% of the antibiotic is released into the environment in the form of its parent or metabolite[3]. The presence of OTC in the aquatic environment is dangerous due to its potential toxicity, carcinogenicity, mutagenicity, and endocrine disruption[4]. In addition, OTC has a highly stable and complex molecular structure that makes it difficult to be biodegraded and causes environmental hazards[5,6]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to find an efficient and economical method to disrupt the complex structure of OTC and remove its biological toxicity.

Currently, physical treatment, biological treatment, and chemical treatment are the mainstream technologies for antibiotic wastewater treatment[7–9]. However, OTC released in the aqueous environment is difficult to remove by conventional treatment processes due to its chemical stability and antibiotic properties[10]. Among them, electrocatalysis is a promising advanced oxidation process with the advantages of cleanliness and low cost that is used to degrade difficult organic compounds[11,12]. Huang et al. prepared MOF-derived CuOx-C electrodes for electrochemical degradation of ceftazidime, and the degradation rate could reach 100% within 20 min under optimal conditions[13]. Man et al. prepared Nd-Sb co-doped SnO2 nanoflower electrodes for the degradation of norfloxacin by the hydrothermal method, and the removal rate was 95.79% after 3 h of reaction. In addition, the E.coil inhibition rate decreased rapidly after 30 min of degradation (from 34.32% to 5.97%) with a significant reduction in biotoxicity[14]. Niu et al. prepared Ti/SnO2-RuO2 stabilized anodes by thermal decomposition and applied them to the electrocatalytic oxidative degradation of cefotaxime sodium (CFX), and the best removal rate of CFX was 86.33%[15]. In conclusion, electrocatalytic technology is highly efficient, flexible, and free from secondary pollution, and is feasible in degrading antibiotics in water[16,17].

The nature of the electrode material is an important parameter in the electrocatalytic treatment of pollutants[18,19]. Among many electrode materials, titanium electrodes are suitable materials for the preparation of electrodes because of their high chemical stability, high surface area, and wide electrochemical potential[20]. Loading active components on Ti substrates yields an effective self-supporting electrode material. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a kind of 0D carbon-dominated nanomaterial that belongs to the graphene family of nanomaterials[21]. Due to their fast electron transfer and high electron storage capacity, loaded CQDs can significantly improve the catalytic performance of the materials[22,23]. In addition, CQDs can be further tuned by heteroatom doping[24]. N atom-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) increase the quantum yield by reducing the energy gap between the π and non-bonded (n) orbitals of carbon, which in turn modulates the electronic structure of CQDs and improves their catalytic performance[25,26]. Therefore, N-CQDs have good electrical conductivity and great potential as an ideal support material for dispersed metal atoms[27–29]. Meanwhile, the dispersion of noble metal nanoparticles on N-CQDs can maximize the noble metal activity and minimize the dosage[30]. The noble metal Pd is capable of activating H2O and H+ and has an incompletely filled d orbital that can produce atomic H* and trap electrons[31–33]. At the same time, the Pd surface can adsorb reaction molecules with moderate adsorption strength, which is conducive to the formation of "activation complexes" and therefore often has relatively high activity.

Inspired by the above analysis, non-metallic N-doped CQDs were synthesized by electrochemical oxidation in this paper. and Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode materials were prepared as electrocatalysts for the degradation of OTC. The objectives of this study were (i) to construct a Pd-N-CQDs interface by preparing Pd/N-CQD/Ti electrodes by electrochemical oxidation and electrodeposition, (ii) to investigate the structure and morphology of the Pd/N-CQD/Ti electrodes, (iii) to probe the mechanism of the generation of atomic hydrogen at the interface of the Pd-N-CQDs, (iv) to explore the performance of the electrocatalytic degradation of OTCs at the Pd/N-CQD/Ti electrodes, and (v) to elucidate the degradation pathway of OTC and to evaluate the toxicity of the degradation intermediates.

The purity of titanium flakes used as electrode material is 99.50%, graphite rods are 99.99%, and platinum flakes are 99.99%. All reagents and chemicals were of analytical grade and were not further purified in the application. All the water used in this experiment was ultrapure.

Preparation of N-CQDs using electrochemical method[25,34]. The graphite rods were first sonicated in ultrapure water for 30 min to remove the surface oxide layer and impurities. Then, two graphite rods were placed in parallel at 4 cm intervals, and the effective length of the graphite rods was 4 cm. Two graphite rods were used as cathode and anode, and the electrolyte solution was 40 mL of 0.1 mol/L NaOH solution, 20 mL of 0.1 mol/L NH3·H2O and 20 mL of ultrapure water. Etching was performed at a constant voltage of 20 V for 2 h. The color of the solution changed from colorless to light gray and finally to brown-black because the anode graphite rod was gradually etched. The obtained brown-black solution was then filtered through a double layer of filter paper three times to remove the precipitated graphite oxide and large particles of graphite. Finally, water-soluble N-CQDs were obtained, dried, and reserved. Ultrasonication is required for 30 min before use to improve the dispersion of N-CQDs and enhance the dispersion of the prepared N-CQDs/Ti support electrode to avoid the subsequent aggregated growth of the loaded metal. The preparation method of CQDs is the same as that of N-CQDs; only the electrolyte solution is different. The electrolyte solution used during the preparation of CQDs is 80 mL of 0.05 mol/L NaOH solution.

The effective working area of the titanium substrate in this experiment was 40 mm × 10 mm. The titanium substrate was degreased by ultrasonication in acetone and ethanol for 15 min each. Then it was cleaned in a mixed solution (Vhydrofluoric acid: Vconcentrated nitric acid: Vwater = 1:4:10) to remove the oil and oxide film on the surface of the titanium sheet, and then rinsed repeatedly with a large amount of ultrapure water. Finally, it was ultrasonically shaken in ultrapure water for 15 min and dried, and set aside. The anodic oxidation method was used to etch the titanium flakes, which is beneficial for the subsequent loading of N-CQDs. The electrolyte was 100 mL of 1.0 mol/L Na2SO4 and 0.5% NaF mixed solution, with a Pt sheet (10 mm × 10 mm) as the cathode and a titanium sheet as the anode. The two electrodes were spaced at 2 cm and etched at 20 V for 1 h. The etched titanium sheet was sonicated for 5 min to remove the surface residue. N-CQDs/Ti supported electrodes were prepared by the cathodic electrodeposition method. The pretreated titanium sheet was used as the cathode and the platinum sheet as the anode with a spacing of 2 cm. 50 mL of 1 mol/L Na2SO4 and 50 mL of N-CQDs with different concentrations were used as deposition solutions. The N-CQDs/Ti support electrode was deposited at a constant current of 5 mA and a constant temperature of 35 °C for 5 min. Finally, the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode was prepared again by the cathodic electrodeposition method again. The N-CQDs/Ti support electrode was used as the cathode, and the Pt sheet was used as the anode. 50 mL of 1 mol/L Na2SO4 and 50 mL of different concentrations of PdCl2 were used as the deposition solutions for 30 min. The constant current was 5 mA and the constant temperature was 35 °C. The fabricated Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode was repeatedly rinsed with deionized water and then set aside.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance analysis of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode
ce:section.ce:para: A field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, XL30 ESEM) equipped with X-ray energy scattering spectroscopy (EDS) was used to investigate the surface morphology of the electrodes, their composition, and their distribution. The operating acceleration voltage was 10 kV, the resolution was 7.6 mm, and the magnification was 10,000 × . The morphology and dimensions of the synthesized CQDs were identified using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200S G2). X-ray diffraction (XRD-7000 s) was performed to determine the crystal structure of the composite electrodes in the 2θ range of 10–80°. The test conditions were as follows: Cu target Kα rays were used as the X-ray source, tube voltage was 40 kV, and tube current was 30 mA. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ThermoFischer, ESCALAB 250Xi) was performed to understand the compositional structure and surface chemical valence of the composite electrodes. Monochromatic Al Kα X-ray sources (1486.6 eV) were used with an operating power of 100 W. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), polarization curves (Tafel), Linear voltammetric scan(LSV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were recorded in a conventional three-electrode system using a Princeton type electrochemical workstation and a 0.5 mol/L Na2SO4 solution. The saturated glycerol electrode and the platinum electrode were used as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The electrochemical impedance spectra were scanned in the frequency range of 105 ∼ 10−2 with a Tafel polarization curve scan rate of 0.167 mV/s and a CV scan range of − 0.6 ∼ 1 V with a scan rate of 50 mV/s.

The prepared Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode was used as the working electrode (cathode) and the platinum sheet as the counter electrode (anode) for the electrocatalytic degradation of oxytetracycline hydrochloride at 35 °C by continuous magnetic stirring. The electrolyte solution was 0.2 mol/L NaCl solution in a volume of 100 mL. The electrocatalytic degradation of 20 mg/L OTC solution was carried out at a constant current of 5 mA with a reaction time of 60 min. The experimental parameters such as electrolyte NaCl concentration, degradation current, and initial OTC concentration were also optimized. To reduce the experimental errors, a programmable DC power supply was selected to provide a constant current for the degradation. And a heat-collecting magnetic stirrer was used to stir uniformly during the experiment to ensure that the whole reaction system was carried out under constant temperature and homogeneous conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apparent morphological analysis of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1(a1-d1) shows the schematic structure of the prepared Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode and corresponds to Fig. 1(a2-d2) one by one. Fig. 1(a2-d2) shows the morphological changes of the titanium substrate surface with oxidation and deposition of N-CQDs and Pd particles using SEM. Fig. 1(a2) shows the surface morphology of the titanium substrate. Some fine lines are present on the surface of the titanium sheet, but it is relatively smooth. The smooth surface is not favorable for the deposition of N-CQDs. Therefore, in this study, the titanium wafer was etched by anodic oxidation to provide more attachment sites for the subsequent loading of N-CQDs and Pd particles. Fig. 1(b2) shows the SEM image of the etched titanium wafer, which shows that the etched titanium substrate surface forms an obvious and more uniform film pore structure. It is beneficial to the subsequent loading of N-CQDs. Fig. 1(c2) shows the surface morphology of the N-CQDs/Ti supported electrode, indicating that N-CQDs are loaded on its surface in the form of agglomerates. N-CQDs not only increase the contact area between the electrode and the electrolyte solution but also participate in the catalytic reaction, providing more active sites for the reaction. Fig. 1(d2) shows that the Pd particles are deposited on the electrode surface, partially covering the membrane pores on the electrode surface with a more uniform distribution. The N-CQDs prepared by the electrochemical method are brown-black (inset of Fig. (e)), and their transmission electron microscopy images (TEM) are shown in Fig. 1(f). It can be seen that the zero-dimensional N-CQDs were successfully prepared in this study with good dispersion and nearly spherical particles with an average particle size of 2.24 nm (inset of Fig. 1(f)). The EDS mapping (Fig. 1(e)) and the elemental distribution map (Fig. 1(g)) confirm the spatial distribution of five elements, C, N, O, Pd, and Ti, on the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode. The results indicate that N-CQDs and Pd particles are successfully deposited on the Ti substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal phase structure and elemental valence analysis of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: XRD tests were used to probe the crystalline phase structure of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode. Fig. 2(a) shows that the main diffraction peaks of Ti appear at 2θ values of 35.00, 38.38, 40.06, 52.88, 62.84, 70.54, 76.12 and 77.38, corresponding to Ti (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (112) and (201) crystallographic planes, respectively. A broad diffraction peak of lower intensity near 2θ of 20.00° shows an amorphous carbon phase, indicating the presence of carbon quantum dots[35]. The broader peak width is due to the thin N-CQDs layer and its poor crystallinity[23]. Pd has a diffraction peak at a 2θ value of 40.06, which belongs to the Pd (111) crystal plane. This can prove the successful loading of Pd. It is noteworthy that the diffraction peak at the 2θ value of 40.06 is the cumulative peak of the Pd (111) crystal plane with the Ti (101) crystal plane.

As shown in Fig. 2(b), the elemental composition and chemical state of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode surface were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which revealed the presence of C, O, N, and Pd elements. Fig. 2(c) shows a high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s that can be fitted to five characteristic peaks. The deconvolution of the spectrum shows that it corresponds to the C-C/CC bond at 284.6 eV. The presence of CC sp2 bonds is characteristic of CQDs[36]. The peak at 284.9 eV is the characteristic peak of the C-N bond, proving the successful introduction of N[37]. The characteristic peaks at 285.4, 286.2, and 288.6 eV correspond to C-OH, C-O/C-O-C, and -COOH bonds, respectively, due to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, in the synthesized carbon quantum dots[25,38,39]. Fig. 2(d) shows the narrow scan spectrum of O 1 s with two characteristic peaks located at 530.1 eV and 531.7 eV, respectively. The peak with relatively lower binding energy represents lattice oxygen (OL), and the peak with relatively higher binding energy represents surface hydroxyl oxygen (Oad)[40]. Functional groups containing heteroatoms (e.g., oxygen and nitrogen) on 0D carbon quantum dots provide ideal active sites for enhanced electrochemical properties[41]. Fig. 2(e) shows the narrow scan spectrum of N 1 s, which can be fitted to four characteristic peaks for distinguishing the type of nitrogen doping. The characteristic peaks located at 398.0, 399.7, 401.1, and 403.1 eV correspond to pyridine N, pyrrole N, graphite N, and oxides of nitrogen, respectively[42]. Among them, pyridine N and graphite N involve sp2 hybridization and are catalytically active, while pyrrole N is associated with sp3 hybridization[26,43]. In addition, pyridine nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen can introduce new defective sites in the carbon quantum dots, thus improving the catalytic activity of 0D carbon quantum dots[44]. The N 1 s coincides with the C 1 s mapping, both of which demonstrate the successful introduction of N and the loading of N-CQDs. Fig. 2(f) shows the narrow scan spectrum of Pd 3d, and it can be seen that two characteristic peaks of Pd 3d5/2 and Pd 3d3/2 of element Pd appear at binding energies of 335.2 eV and 340.5 eV[45]. The results show the successful loading of Pd on the electrode surface and also demonstrate that the Pd loaded on the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode is zero-valent palladium.

When metal particles and carbon carriers have different Fermi energy levels, electron transfer at the interface has been reported to occur, which manifests itself as electronic metal-carbon interaction (EMCI). The metal clusters are more inclined to interact with the positions where the heteroatoms undergo doped-induced electron delocalization[46]. To verify this role, we compared the XPS patterns of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti, N-CQDs/Ti, and Pd/Ti electrodes. In the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode, the N species binding energies (BEs) were changed and showed a negative shift compared to the N-CQDs/Ti electrode ( Fig. 3e). In contrast, the binding energy of Pd 3d5/2 was positively shifted from 335.0 eV for Pd/Ti to 335.2 eV for Pd/N-CQDs/Ti, with a positive shift of 0.2 eV. These results suggest that the N-CQDs interacted with the Pd particles, and the electrons were transferred from the Pd particles to the N species (Fig. 3f)[47]. In the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode, the center of nitrogen has a higher electron density due to the higher electronegativity of N than carbon, which can be recovered from the carbon atoms[48]. And in EMCI, the N atom acts as an electron acceptor and Pd acts as an electron donor, forming a Pd-N interface, which in turn improves its catalytic activity.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was used to investigate the electrochemical reaction mechanism and charge transfer rate. Fig. 3(a) shows the Nyquist plots of the Ti substrate, N-CQDs/Ti support electrode, and Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode. The series resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), and double layer capacitance (Cdl) were obtained using an equivalent circuit. Where Rs is the resistance of the electrolyte and the active material, a smaller Rs means that the composite electrode has a larger specific surface area and electron transfer channel. The smaller Rct represents a higher electron exchange capacity between the electrode and the electrolyte, i.e., a stronger electrocatalytic activity[49]. Cdl represents the charge storage capacity of the electrode-solution interface at a certain potential perturbation. A larger Cdl value means that the electrode has more reactive sites[50]. The relevant kinetic parameters, obtained by equivalent circuit fitting, are listed in Table 1. As expected, the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode has the smallest RS (2.296 Ω cm2), the smallest Rct (757.9 Ω cm2), and the largest Cdl (2.224 × 10−2 F cm−2). Thus, the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode is more favorable for electron transfer while having the smallest charge transfer resistance and the most reactive sites. Notably, the charge transfer resistance of the Ti substrate, N-CQDs/Ti support electrode, and Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode decreased sequentially, proving that Pd and N-CQDs have a synergistic effect, resulting in a significant increase in the electrocatalytic activity of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode.

Fig. 3(b) shows the polarization curves of the three electrodes. Under the same conditions, the more positive the hydrogen precipitation potential of the electrodes, the better the hydrogen precipitation activity and the better the electrocatalytic performance[51]. It can be seen from Fig. 3(b) that both Pd/N-CQDs/Ti and N-CQDs/Ti electrodes have more positive hydrogen precipitation potential than the Ti electrode at the same current density. The Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode is relatively more likely to undergo a positive shift. Therefore, the loading of N-CQDs and Pd is beneficial to improving the catalytic activity.

To further evaluate the electrode's ability to generate H* , linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was performed without OTC, as shown in Fig. 3(c). The Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode exhibited the strongest current (−86.66 mA) at 1.4 V vs. SCE, 1.36 times and 5.45 times the N-CQDs/Ti and Ti electrodes, respectively. The initial potential of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode is the first positive shift, followed by the N-CQDs/Ti electrode. The results show that Pd and N-CQDs can confer a higher H* generation rate on the electrode. Moreover, electrons can be transferred from the Pd state to the N state, thus forming a Pd-N interface, which is favorable for the generation of atomic hydrogen.

Cyclic voltammetry tests were performed for Ti, N-CQDs, and Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes, as shown in Fig. 3(d). It can be seen that the electrode after loading N-CQDs and Pd has a larger ring than the pure titanium sheet. The Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode has the largest CV curve area and presents a large electrochemical active region, indicating that the loading of N-CQDs and Pd is beneficial to regulate the electron density on the electrode surface and improve the catalytic performance of the electrode[52]. The results are consistent with the results of EIS, polarization curve tests and LSV, both of which indicate that the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode has the best electrocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of electrode preparation conditions on the performance of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of N-CQDs during the deposition process has an important effect on the electrode’s performance. The concentration of N-CQDs was optimized using the control variable method to determine the optimal concentration. The effect of N-CQDs concentration on electrode performance is shown in Fig. 4(a). When the concentration of N-CQDs deposition solution was increased from 0 mg/L to 40 mg/L, the removal of OTC was the first to increase and then to decrease. This is because as the initial concentration of N-CQDs increases, more N-CQDs are loaded onto the surface of the titanium substrate, which enhances the catalytic activity of the electrode. However, when the concentration of N-CQDs was too high, it made the electrode surface overloaded with N-CQDs and the active sites covered, so the degradation effect decreased instead. Among them, when the concentration of N-CQDs was 10 mg/L, the highest removal rate of OTC could reach 65.20%. Fig. 4(b) shows that the degradation of OTC by electrodes with different N-CQDs concentrations is in accordance with the Langmuir-Hinshelwood proposed primary reaction kinetics. Among them, the largest reaction rate constant of 0.01743 min−1 was observed for N-CQDs at 10 mg/L. Therefore, the N-CQDs concentration of 10 mg/L was determined to be the optimal concentration for the preparation of N-CQDs/Ti supported electrodes.

The amount of palladium loading affects the surface morphology and catalytic activity of the electrode, and an appropriate amount of palladium loading will achieve high catalytic efficiency. In this experiment, different concentrations of PdCl2 deposition solutions were prepared to prepare Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrodes with different Pd loadings to investigate the optimal concentration of PdCl2 in the deposition solution. A series of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrodes were prepared by loading palladium on the N-CQDs/Ti support electrode prepared by selecting the optimal N-CQDs concentration. The OTC was degraded at a constant current of 5 mA, and the results are shown in Fig. 4(c). The degradation efficiency of oxytetracycline hydrochloride was 68.21%, 71.33%, 66.11%, 65.42% and 64.10% when the PdCl2 concentration was 5, 10, 15, 20 and 25 μmol/L, respectively. The removal rate of OTC increased and then decreased with increasing PdCl2 concentration. The reaction rate constant k increased from 0.01717 min−1 to 0.02114 min−1 (Fig. 4d). The increase in k can be explained by the reduction of OTC molecules through the production of more reactive atomic hydrogen with the increase in Pd loading. However, when the PdCl2 concentration was increased to 15 μmol/L, the Pd loading was excessive and had an inhibitory effect on the degradation of OTC. This can be attributed to the decrease in efficiency of the reduction of OTC by the active atomic hydrogen due to the excessive loading of Pd[45]. Therefore, the optimal PdCl2 concentration was determined to be 10 μmol/L.

In this study, the undoped N carbon quantum dots/titanium supported electrode (CQDs/Ti) and Pd/Ti electrode were prepared according to the optimized optimal N-CQDs concentration and PdCl2 concentration. And the performance of different electrodes for OTC degradation was compared, as shown in Fig. 4(e). The modification effect of N doping on zero-dimensional CQDs and the synergistic effect of N-CQDS recombination with Pd particles were studied. As seen in Fig. 4(e), the degradation efficiency of CQDs/Ti and Pd/Ti electrodes for OTC was higher than that of pure titanium flakes, indicating the catalytic activity of both CQDs and Pd particles. The degradation efficiency of the N-CQDs/Ti electrode is 3.69% higher than that of the CQDs/Ti electrode, indicating that the doping of N atoms in the 0D carbon quantum dot structure provides more active sites for improving the electrochemical behavior. Combined with Fig. 2(d), N doping not only modulates the electronic structure of CQDs, but also forms graphitic N and pyridine N structures, thus significantly improving the catalyst activity[53]. Notably, the best degradation efficiency of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode for OTC can reach 71.33%, which is 6.13% and 9.40% higher than that of the N-CQDs/Ti electrode and Pd/Ti electrode, respectively. The results show that there is a synergistic effect between Pd and N-CQDs, which is consistent with the electrochemical performance analysis results. Introducing N atoms and complexing them with Pd particles can form a Pd-N-CQDs interface to further improve the catalytic activity of zero-dimensional CQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 4(f), the degradation of OTC by different electrodes is consistent with the proposed primary reaction kinetics. Among them, the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode had the largest reaction rate constant (k = 0.02114 min−1) for the degradation of OTC, indicating that the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode had the fastest degradation rate. Therefore, the catalytic activity of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode was superior to that of the other four electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of degradation conditions on the performance of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of the electrolyte affects the conductivity of the solution and the energy consumption during the removal of contaminants. In this experiment, the effect of the concentration of the electrolyte NaCl solution on the degradation of hyoscyamine hydrochloride by the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode was investigated, as shown in Fig. 5(a). The Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode prepared at the optimal N-CQDs concentration and PdCl2 concentration was used as the working electrode in the subsequent experiments. The removal effect of the composite electrode on OTC was examined at NaCl concentrations ranging from 0.10 mol/L to 0.50 mol/L. The degradation efficiency of oxytetracycline hydrochloride increased continuously with the increase in electrolyte concentration, up to 82.73%. The reason for this is that an increase in electrolyte concentration increases the probability of collision between particles in the reaction system, thus increasing the removal rate[54]. The results of the degradation of OTC by the composite electrode at different NaCl concentrations were linearly fitted, and the fitted results are shown in Fig. 5(b). The degradation of OTC by the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode at different electrolyte NaCl concentrations was consistent with the proposed the first order reaction kinetics. The reaction rate constants for the degradation of OTC by the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode were, in descending order, as follows: 0.50 mol/L (0.02757 min−1) > 0.40 mol/L (0.02751 min−1) > 0.30 mol/L (0.02346 min−1) > 0.20 mol/L (0.02114 min−1) > 0.15 mol/L (0.01511 min−1) > 0.10 mol/L (0.00458 min−1). When the NaCl concentration was 0.5 mol/L, the reaction rate of the composite electrode to degrade OTC was slightly higher than that at 0.4 mol/L, but the difference in the final degradation efficiency of OTC between the two was only 3.38%. Therefore, the optimal concentration of the electrolyte NaCl solution was chosen to be 0.40 mol/L from the viewpoint of economic practicality.

In electrochemical reaction systems, the magnitude of the current determines the energy consumption and removal efficiency of the degradation reaction. In this experiment, the effect of different degradation currents on the performance of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode was investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 5(c). When the working current is 9 mA, the highest degradation efficiency of 89.92% can be achieved with a reaction time of 60 min. When the operating current was lower than 9 mA, the removal efficiency of OTC increased with the increase in current. This is due to the fact that when the current is too low, it prolongs the electrochemical catalytic reaction time and reduces the degradation effect. However, when the operating current reaches 11 mA, the degradation efficiency decreases instead. The reason is that too high current causes the side reaction of electrolytic water to occur, and a large number of bubbles appear near the cathode. It has a strong stripping effect on the loading layer of the electrode surface, leading to the peeling of the electrode surface and a decrease in the degradation efficiency of the electrode decreases. Therefore, 9 mA is the optimum operating current. As shown in Fig. 5(d), when the operating current is 9 mA, the reaction rate constant is the largest at 0.03424 min−1, indicating the fastest reaction rate. Therefore, 9 mA was determined to be the optimal operating current.

In this study, the effect of different initial OTC concentrations on the degradation effect of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes was investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 5(e). In the degradation experiments, the initial concentrations of OTC were 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 20, 40, and 60 mg/L. It can be seen that the degradation efficiency increases with the increase in the initial concentration when the OTC concentration is lower than 20 mg/L. The degradation efficiency decreases with the increase in the initial concentration when the OTC concentration is higher than 20 mg/L. The best degradation efficiency of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode can reach 89.92% at 20 mg/L. There are two reasons for this: one is that the lower initial OTC concentration limits the diffusion of OTC molecules, thus weakening their binding to oxidizing or reducing radicals. Therefore, degradation efficiency is limited. The other is that at high OTC concentrations, OTC molecules and their degradation intermediates occupy the reactive sites of the electrodes. They have a competitive effect, which in turn leads to a decrease in degradation efficiency[55]. Combined with the inset in Fig. 5(e), it can be found that the k value is the largest when the initial concentration of OTC is 20 mg/L, which is 0.03424 min−1. This proves that the best degradation effect and the fastest rate are achieved at the initial concentration of 20 mg/L. Subsequent experiments were conducted with the initial OTC concentration of 20 mg/L.

The initial pH of the solution has an important effect on the generation of reactive oxygen species and the surface charge properties, which in turn affect the catalytic degradation rate. In this experiment, the effect of different initial pH values on the degradation efficiency of OTC was investigated, as shown in Fig. 5(g). The degradation efficiency was 70.68%, 82.90%, 89.92%, 76.63%, 73.34%, 72.90% and 70.47%, respectively. As the pH increased, the OTC removal efficiency first increased and then decreased. When the pH was 4.5, the best degradation efficiency was 89.92%. When the pH was too low, the effective layer on the electrode surface was peeled off, leading to a significant decrease in degradation efficiency. When the pH is too high, the production of·OH will be inhibited, which in turn leads to a decrease in efficiency. To further investigate the effect of pH on the surface charge characteristics of the electrode and the morphology of OTC presence, the determination of the zero charge point (pHpzc) was carried out. The composite electrode was immersed in 50 mL of 0.1 mol/L NaCl solution, and the initial pH of the solution (pHi) was adjusted to 4 ∼ 10. The solution pH (pHf) was determined after shaking for 24 h. The results are shown in Fig. 5(f). pHpzc of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode was 6.12. The results indicate that when the solution pH < 6.12, the electrode surface protonats with a positive charge. When pH > 6.12, the electrode surface deprotonates with a negative charge. Meanwhile, OTC has a different morphological distribution at different pH. When the pH ≤ 3.57, OTC exists in the cationic form (H3OTC+). When the pH is between 3.57 and 7.49, OTC exists mainly as amphoteric ions (H2OTC±). When the pH is between 7.49 and 9.44, OTC exists mainly in the form of anions (HOTC-). When the pH > 9.44, OTC shows a double anionic form (OTC2-)[56]. Based on the principle that homogeneous charges repel each other and heterogeneous charges attract each other, the optimal pH for degrading OTC was determined to be between 3.57 and 7.49. The optimal initial pH of the solution was determined to be 4.5 in combination with Fig. 5(g).

Temperature affects the conductivity of the solution during electrocatalysis as well as the thermal movement of the molecules. It is crucial to investigate the effect of reaction temperature on the degradation effect of OTC. The reaction temperatures were set at 20 °C, 25 °C, 30 °C, 35 °C, and 40 °C, and the results are shown in Fig. 5(h). The degradation efficiency of OTC gradually decreased with increasing temperature from 20 °C to 40 °C. This phenomenon is attributed to the decrease in the conductivity of the solution with increasing temperature[57]. At the same time, high temperatures accelerate molecular collisions, leading to accelerated side reactions and reducing current efficiency and·OH utilization[58]. When the temperature was 20 °C and 25 °C, the degradation efficiency was 90.52% and 90.41%, respectively, with little difference. Considering the energy consumption, 25 °C at room temperature was chosen as the optimal temperature for degrading OTC.

The degradation efficiency, Pd concentration, and degradation time of the present study were compared with the literature (Fig. 5i)[59–63]. The degradation rate of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode was higher than most of the literature, and the Pd concentration was lower. The above results emphasize the improvement of Pd activity and the reduction of dosage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and universality of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the reusability of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode, 10 consecutive electrochemical degradation experiments were performed on the electrode. As can be seen in Fig. 6(a), the degradation efficiency of the electrode decreased from 90.41% to 88.36% after 10 consecutive cycles. The degradation efficiency only decreased by 2.05% after ten cycles, which was basically unchanged. This is supposed to be due to the electronic interactions between N-CQDs and Pd, which can anchor Pd particles and reduce the agglomeration and leaching of Pd particles. In addition, the XRD patterns of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode before and after the degradation of hyoscyamine hydrochloride were investigated. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the XRD patterns were not significantly different. The results indicate that the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode prepared in this study not only has high electrocatalytic activity and stability but also has the possibility of practical application.

To further investigate the pervasiveness of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode, degradation experiments with different antibiotics were conducted, such as tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), tylosin (TYL), doxycycline hydrochloride (DOX), norfloxacin (NOR) and sulfamethoxazole (SMX). As shown in Fig. 6(c), the removal efficiency of the composite electrode for TC (89.73%) was second only to the degradation rate of OTC, while the degradation rates for other antibiotics were η (TYL) = 72.81%, η (DOX) = 69.55%, η (NOR) = 65.54%, and η (SMX) = 44.72%. The results indicate that the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti composite electrode prepared in this work is widely applicable to the degradation of antibiotics.

This study tests the performance of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes in real water bodies, including tap water and Songhua River water. As shown in Fig. 6(d), the degradation rates of OTC in tap water and Songhua River water were 87.74% and 87.90% after 60 min of reaction, respectively. The degradation effect in ambient water was slightly decreased compared with that in ultrapure water. The decrease in degradation effect may be due to the unavoidable competing substances in the actual wastewater, such as inorganic ions and dissolved organic substances[64]. Meanwhile, the initial pH of tap water and Songhua River water was 6.99 and 7.09, respectively, and the degradation efficiency decreased probably because the pH of both was close to neutral[65]. The results show that Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes are promising for the remediation of real environmental wastewater.

In addition, some previous reports on OTC degradation were summarized and compared with the efficiency of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes for OTC degradation, as shown in Table 2 [66–70]. Compared with the reported catalysts, the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode showed excellent efficiency in degrading OTC in a short time. The results further demonstrate that the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC by the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode is a promising treatment method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic analysis of OTC degradation by Pd/N-CQDs/Ti
ce:section.ce:para:
Electrocatalytic oxidation for the degradation of pollutants can be divided into direct and indirect oxidation, where·OH can participate in the degradation of organic matter through indirect oxidation[71]. To confirm the production of·OH during the degradation of OTC at the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode, coumarin was selected as a fluorescent probe added to the electrochemical reaction cell. Since coumarin reacts with·OH to produce 7-hydroxycoumarin with fluorescent properties, fluorescence spectra can be obtained using molecular fluorescence photometry. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of 7-hydroxycoumarin produced in solution was used to compare the concentration of·OH production catalyzed by the electrode at different degradation times. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the fluorescence intensity of 7-hydroxycoumarin gradually increased with the increase in electrocatalytic time, indicating that the content of·OH gradually increased. Therefore, there is·OH generation during the electrocatalytic reaction, which is used for the oxidative degradation of OTC. The inhibitors can prevent the electrode from producing active substances. In this study, two inhibitors, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), were selected at a concentration of 1 mol/L. Usually, IPA is considered to detect the·OH generated during catalytic oxidation, while TBA is a scavenger for the detection of·OH and H* . Therefore, this study further verified the indirect oxidation of·OH by quenching experiments, and the results are shown in Fig. 7(b). When the IPA concentration was 1 mol/L, the removal rate of OTC decreased from 90.41% to 78.82% in 60 min, indicating the presence of·OH in the electrocatalytic process for indirect oxidation of OTC. The specific process is shown in Eq. 1 and Eq. 2. Through the anodic oxidation of water, non-homogeneous·OH is generated and adsorbed on the anode surface (Eq. 1)[20]. The adsorbed state of·OH further oxidized and degraded the OTC molecules (Eq. 2). About 78% of the OTC was still removed 60 min after the addition of IPA, which proves that a direct oxidation mechanism may exist in the degradation process. Direct oxidation is achieved by direct electron transfer between the OTC adsorbed on the anode surface and the active layer on the electrode surface (Eq. 3)[49]. However, after the addition of TBA, the degradation efficiency decreased to 71.54%, which was 7.28% lower than after the addition of IPA, proving that H* also contributed to the overall system.

Where [·OH]ads represents the adsorbed state of the hydroxyl radical and M represents the anode.

Since the electrolyte for the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC is NaCl, the anode may produce active chlorine species. To verify this inference, linear sweep scans were performed for the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode in different electrolyte solutions. By adding NaCl to the Na2SO4 solution, it was determined whether the LSV response was attributed to the oxygen precipitation reaction or to the chlorine precipitation process. As shown in Fig. 7(c), the oxygen precipitation reaction occurs on the anode surface in Na2SO4 solution with an oxygen precipitation potential of about 1.12 V vs. SCE. In addition, the starting potential gradually decreases with the increase in Cl- concentration[72]. This is because the precipitation chlorine potential is lower than the precipitation oxygen potential, and a large amount of Cl- will replace the water molecules as they oxidize on the anode surface. The results show that active chlorine is produced during the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC. The precipitation of active chlorine is accomplished by a direct oxidation reaction on the anode surface (Eqs. 4 and 5)[73]. Different pH values affect the production of active chlorine, which exists as strongly oxidizing HClO under acidic conditions (Eq. 6). Under alkaline conditions, it exists as weakly oxidizing ClO- (Eq. 7), which affects degradation[74]. The reaction solution in this study was acidic, so the active chlorine was mainly in the form of HClO.

Since the cathode and the anode are in the same system in this study, the degradation of OTC is catalyzed by both the anode and the cathode[51,75]. Therefore, reduction will also occur at and near the cathode surface. It is noteworthy that the pathway of H2O2 generation on the cathode surface for OTC degradation is often overlooked. In this study, the iodometric method was used to detect the concentration of H2O2 in the reaction solution to demonstrate the contribution of H2O2 generated on the cathode to the OTC degradation. As shown in Fig. 7(d), the concentration of H2O2 in the solution gradually increased with the increase of degradation time to 0.180 mmol/L. The results indicated that the H2O2 degradation process of OTC existed in this experiment.

Fig. 7(e) shows the reaction mechanism on the surface of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode. The electrochemical reduction includes direct reduction and indirect reduction. Among them, the direct reduction is achieved by direct electron transfer (Eq. 8 and Fig. 7(e) step ③)[76]. Indirect reduction is the reduction of OTC molecules using H* generated on the cathode surface[77]. Atomic hydrogen is usually generated on the palladium-based cathode surface by the reduction of protons (H+) or water molecules, i.e., the Volmer step (Eq. 9 and Fig. 7(e) step ①)[29]. The high affinity of Pd for adsorbed hydrogen and the high generation and retention efficiency of H* is achieved by forming a Pd-H bond consisting of a σ bond between the H atom and the d orbital of the Pd atom[78,79]. OTC molecules and their degradation intermediates can be adsorbed on the cathode surface and then H* acts as the key reducing agent to degrade them (Eq. 10 and Eq. 11, Fig. 7(e) step ②). The reduction products generated after degradation are desorbed and thus enter the solution (Eq. 12). Finally, since the generation of H2 requires a more negative potential than that of H* , H* is rapidly converted to H2 via the Heyrovsky and Tafel pathways (Eq. 13 and Eq. 14, steps ④ and ⑤ of Fig. 7e), i.e., the hydrogen precipitation side reaction[80,81]. As intermediates, [H* ]ads can diffuse into the lattice of Pd to form H* adsorbed in the lattice ([H*]abs), which can be used in subsequent reduction reactions[78]. In addition, H2O2 is generated in situ at the cathode by a two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) (Eq. 15, step ⑥ of Fig. 7e)·H2O2 is reduced by one electron to·OH, which can further oxidize the OTC (Eq. 16, step ⑦ of Fig. 7e)[82]. Therefore, the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode (cathode) prepared in this study can co-catalyze the degradation of OTC through the synergistic action of the cathode and anode.

To further clarify the degradation process of OTC, the degradation products during the degradation of OTC were examined by LC-MS, and the results are shown in Fig. S1 and Table S1. According to the oxidation of·OH, the reduction of H* , and the ring-opening of the benzene ring by HClO, three possible pathways of OTC degradation were deduced, as shown in Fig. 7(f). The first degradation pathway is demethylation. The methyl group in the dimethylamino group of the OTC molecule is substituted with hydrogen to give P1 (m/z = 447). P1 (m/z = 447) removes the formyl group and another methyl group in the dimethylamino group to produce P2 (m/z = 390). Then the P3 (m/z = 348) is generated from P2 (m/z = 390) after demethylation, hydroxyl and ketone groups, and reduction of a CC. Then P3 (m/z = 348) C-C bond is broken to give P4 (m/z = 279). P4 (m/z = 279) is further generated as P5 (m/z = 141) by dehydroxylation, and C-C bond breakage. The second degradation pathway is the deamidation group. The OTC molecule is oxidized to remove the amide group to give intermediate P6 (m/z = 418). P6 (m/z = 418) undergoes the removal of methyl and dimethylamino groups and breaks C-C to give P7 (m/z = 337). P7 (m/z = 337) undergoes ring-opening cleavage, dehydroxylation, and oxidation of the hydroxyl group to a carbonyl group to give P8 (m/z = 223). The third degradation pathway is hydroxylation. P9 (m/z = 477) is a by-product of OTC hydroxylation. By further disproportionation reaction, P9 (m/z = 477) was converted to quinone by-product P10 (m/z = 475). P10 (m/z = 475) was demethylated from the amide group and two methyl groups in dimethylamine to produce P11 (m/z = 404). After demethylation, dehydroxylation, C-C break and oxidation of the hydroxyl group to the carbonyl group and reduction of CC to C-C, P12 (m/z = 263) was produced. P12 (m/z = 263) undergoes decarbonylation and oxidation of the carbonyl group to the carboxyl group to produce P13 (m/z = 245). The intermediate products in the degradation pathway, P5 (m/z = 141), P8 (m/z = 223), and P13 (m/z = 245), are gradually decomposed into small molecules such as P14 (m/z = 119), P15 (m/z = 117) and P16 (m/z = 101). Finally, these small molecules are further mineralized into CO2 and H2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity assessment of Pd/N-CQDs/Ti degraded OTC
ce:section.ce:para: The environmental risk of OTC degradation by the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode was further investigated by predicting the acute toxicity and chronic toxicity of degradation products to fish, daphnia and green algae during electrocatalytic degradation of OTC by ECOSAR 2.2 software. The results are shown in Fig. 8(a) and (b) and Table S2. From Fig. 8(a), it can be seen that the acute toxicity of OTC in the water column is not harmful to fish, toxic to daphnia and harmful to green algae. After catalytic degradation by Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes, the acute toxicity of P4, P5 and P11 to fish increased. However, with the degradation reaction the three products could be transformed into less toxic small molecules P14, P15 and P16, which finally reached non-toxicity. P2 and P3 were more acutely toxic to Daphnia than OTC, and the rest of the products were less toxic than OTC itself. However, P2 and P3 can be further converted to P4 with reduced toxicity. The acute toxicity of P2, P3, P4 and P5 to green algae is higher, and the toxicity of P11 increases sharply, but the five products can be further converted from P14, P15 and P16, which are ultimately non-toxic. It can be seen in Fig. 8(b) that OTC is not chronically toxic to fish, daphnia and green algae. The chronic toxicity of OTC degradation intermediates to fish, daphnia and green algae is consistent with the acute toxicity pattern, and they can all be eventually converted to non-toxic P14, P15 and P16. Therefore, the degradation of OTC by Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes is a green and reliable method.

Toxicity assessment software (TEST) was used to make predictions based on quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) to evaluate bioaccumulation factors (BCFs), developmental toxicity, and mutagenicity of OTC and its degradation intermediates, as shown in Fig. 8(c-e) and Table S3. Fig. 8(c) shows the BFCs of OTC and its degradation intermediates. The test prediction program could not obtain BFCs for P7, P10, P12, and P14 due to the absence of corresponding experimental data. Most of these intermediates have significantly reduced bioaccumulation factors, with P2 and P9 having greater bioaccumulation factors than maternal OTC, but P2 and P9 can be further converted into P3 and P11 with smaller BFCs. According to Fig. 8(d), most intermediates have lower developmental toxicity than maternal OTC; only P12 is more toxic than OTC; and all intermediates are still classified as having “developmental toxicity”. In Fig. 8(e), OTC has "positive mutagenicity", and the mutagenicity values of P5, P8, P10, P11, P13, P14, P15 and P16 of the products after Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrocatalytic degradation are all reduced to “negative mutagenicity”. Therefore, it can be concluded that OTC is degraded into low-toxic compounds.

Energy consumption is one of the most important factors when evaluating the feasibility of the practical application of electrochemical treatment. The energy consumption per stage (EEO; kWh/m3) is used to evaluate the energy consumption in the OTC degradation process and is calculated as follows (Eq. 17)[83].
(17)
EEO =
E
cell
It

log ( C

)
Where E
cell is the electrolytic cell potential (V), I is the current (A), t is the treatment time (h), V is the volume of treated water (m3), and C
0 and C
t are the initial and final OTC concentrations (mg/L), respectively.

In this study, the energy consumption for OTC degradation was compared with that previously reported for electrocatalytic degradation of other pollutants, including ciprofloxacin (CIP), nitrobenzene (NB), and acid brown (AB-14), as shown in Table 3 [80,84–86]. In this study, the energy consumption required for the OTC degradation efficiency of 90.41% was 0.288 kWh/m3, which was the lowest compared to previous literature. This indicates that the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode degrades OTC with low energy consumption, thus saving energy costs. Therefore, the electrode is an efficient and low energy-consuming electrode for OTC degradation.

In this study, Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrodes were successfully prepared and showed excellent electrocatalytic activity for OTC removal. At natural pH, the OTC removal rate reached 90.41% within 60 min of the reaction, and the energy consumption was only 0.288 kWh/m3. Meanwhile, the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode showed reasonable stability after 10 cycling experiments. The formation of a Pd-N interface between N-CQDs and Pd at this electrode is responsible for the targeted electron transfer from Pd to N-CQDs. The high activity of the Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode is mainly attributed to the synergistic interaction between the zero-dimensional N-CQDs and Pd. In addition, the degradation mechanism of OTC is proposed, i.e.,·OH, H* , and HClO are involved in the degradation. The Pd/N-CQDs/Ti electrode still maintains good reactivity in the degradation of OTC in real water bodies (87.74% in tap water and 87.90% in Songhua River water). ECOSAR and TEST evaluations show that the electrode is effective in decreasing the biotoxicity of OTC. This work provides new insights into the remediation of antibiotic-contaminated water.

Jian Zhang: Formal analysis, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Junlian Ren: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Validation, Methodology, Visualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Fanjin Wang: Formal analysis. Lanhe Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Bing Wang: Data curation, Supervision. Weihua He: Formal analysis, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.
